writing.task1: The chart shows how many households in a European country owned four electronic devices from 2000 to 2020. Overall, all four items became more common over time, but some grew faster than others. In 2000, televisions had the highest ownership at about 90%, and this number slowly increased until almost every household had one by 2020. Computers started at around 40%, and the percentage rose steadily to roughly 80% at the end of the period. Mobile phones showed the biggest growth. Only about 30% of households owned a mobile phone in 2000, but this number increased very quickly and reached almost 100% by 2020. Internet access also grew sharply. It began at only around 10% in 2000, but more than 80% of households had internet by 2020. In general, televisions stayed the most common device, while mobile phones and internet connections increased the fastest.
writing.task2: Some people think governments should spend more money on public transportation because it can reduce traffic jams, pollution, and travel costs for many people. When buses, trains, and metros are cheap, safe, and easy to use, more citizens will choose them instead of driving, which helps the environment and makes cities less crowded. Public transport also supports people who cannot afford cars, so it creates more equality in society. However, others believe improving roads is more important because many areas still do not have good road networks, especially in rural places. Better roads can make transportation of goods easier, help emergency services reach people faster, and support economic growth. Roads are also needed for buses, trucks, and cars, so upgrading them can benefit everyone, not only drivers. In my opinion, both are necessary, but governments should focus more on public transport because it brings long-term benefits and solves problems like air pollution and traffic jams more effectively. Roads should still be maintained, but public transportation deserves a larger share of the budget to create cleaner, safer, and more convenient cities for the future.

sections.title: Listening
sections.description: 40 questions • Approximately 30 minutes • You will hear each recording only once

parts.title: Part 1: Conversation about renting accommodation
parts.instructions: Listen to the conversation and answer questions 1-10.

script.narrator:
You will hear a conversation between a student and a university accommodation officer. First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 10.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 10.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Good morning, University Accommodation Office, how can I help you?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Hello, I'm looking for accommodation for next semester. I saw online that you have different options available.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Yes, we have several choices. First, could I get your student ID number?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: It's ST20478.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Thank you. Now, let me explain our main options. We have university halls, shared houses, and studio apartments.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: What's the difference in price between these options?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: University halls start at £120 per week, shared houses are around £95-£110, and studios are £150-£180 per week.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: And what's included in these prices?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: All include utilities and internet. The halls also include cleaning of common areas, while the other options don't.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: How about location? I prefer to be close to the Business School.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: The university halls are about 5 minutes walk from there. The shared houses are 10-15 minutes, and studios are 8 minutes away.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: What about public transport? I might need to get to the city centre sometimes.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: There's a bus stop right outside the halls. Buses run every 10 minutes to the city centre.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Are there any additional costs I should know about?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: You'll need to pay a £250 deposit, which is refundable when you leave if there's no damage. Also, there's a £50 administration fee.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: When is the payment due?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: We require the first month's rent and deposit when you sign the contract. After that, rent is due on the first of each month.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Can I see some photos of the accommodation?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Of course. I can email you our accommodation brochure with photos and floor plans. What's your email address?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: It's tom.lee@student.university.ac.uk

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Thank you. I'll send that right away. Would you like to book a viewing for next week?

questions.questionText: What is the student's ID number?
How much do shared houses cost per week?
What is included in all accommodation options?
How far is the Business School from university halls?
How often do buses run to the city centre?
How much is the deposit?
When is rent due each month?
options: A) Last day of the month
D) First Monday of the month

questions.questionText: What will the accommodation officer email?
What is the student's email address?
What does the officer suggest booking?

parts.title: Part 2: Talk about library facilities
parts.instructions: Listen to the talk and answer questions 11-20.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 2. You will hear a talk about university library facilities. Look at questions 11 to 20.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 20.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Good afternoon and welcome to the university library orientation. I'm Sarah, one of the senior librarians, and I'm here to explain our facilities and services. The library is open from 8am to 10pm Monday to Friday, and 9am to 8pm on weekends. During exam periods, we extend our hours until midnight. To borrow books, you'll need your student card. Undergraduate students can borrow up to 10 items for two weeks, while postgraduate students can borrow 15 items for four weeks. You can renew books online up to three times, unless another student has reserved them. Our library has four floors. The ground floor contains the reception desk, newspapers, and popular fiction. The first floor has social sciences books and group study rooms. The second floor is for sciences and engineering, and the third floor contains quiet study areas and our special collections. We have 150 computers available for student use, all with internet access and printing facilities. Printing costs 5p per black and white page and 15p for colour. You'll need to top up your printing credit at the machine near reception. We also offer laptop loans for up to four hours - just ask at the desk. Our online resources include access to academic journals, e-books, and databases. You can access these from anywhere using your university login. Finally, we run workshops throughout the semester on research skills, referencing, and using specific databases. You can book these online through the library website. Are there any questions?

questions.questionText: What time does the library close on weekdays?
How many items can undergraduate students borrow?
How long can postgraduate students keep books?
Where are the group study rooms located?
How much does colour printing cost per page?
Where can students top up printing credit?
options: D) At a machine near reception

questions.questionText: How long can laptops be borrowed for?
What is needed to access online resources from home?
What type of workshops are mentioned?
How can students book workshops?

parts.title: Part 3: Discussion about a university project
parts.instructions: Listen to the discussion and answer questions 21-30.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 3. You will hear two students discussing a group project. Look at questions 21 to 30.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 30.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Hi Mark, have you started working on the environmental science project yet?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Not really, Maria. I've been focusing on my economics assignment. When is it due exactly?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: It's due in three weeks, on the 15th of November. But we need to submit a project plan by this Friday.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Oh right. What's the project about again? I missed the last lecture.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: We have to choose one environmental issue and analyze both its causes and potential solutions. I was thinking about plastic pollution in oceans.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: That's a good topic. Should we focus on a specific region or keep it general?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: I think we should focus on Southeast Asia, since that's where a lot of ocean plastic comes from. We could look at countries like Indonesia and the Philippines.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Okay, that sounds manageable. How should we divide the work?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Well, I could research the causes and current situation, while you look at existing solutions and their effectiveness. Then we can both work on recommendations.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: That works for me. Should we use any specific research methods?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: I think we should use a combination of academic articles and recent reports from environmental organizations. Dr. Roberts mentioned we need at least 10 references.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Do we have to include any primary research?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: No, secondary research is fine for this project. But we should include statistics and data to support our analysis.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: When should we meet to combine our research?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: How about next Wednesday afternoon? That gives us a week to do individual research first.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Wednesday works for me. Should we meet in the library or somewhere else?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Let's meet in the group study room on the first floor of the library. I'll book it for 2pm.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Perfect. I'll start my research tomorrow after my economics tutorial.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Great. Don't forget we need to email our project plan to Dr. Roberts by 5pm on Friday.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: I'll make sure to contribute to that. Thanks for organizing everything, Maria.

questions.questionText: What subject is the project for?
When is the project due?
What is the first deadline mentioned?
options: D) First draft in two weeks

questions.questionText: What specific topic do they choose?
Which region will they focus on?
What part will Mark research?
How many references are required?
What type of research is required?
When will they meet next?
Where will they meet?

parts.title: Part 4: Lecture on urban planning
parts.instructions: Listen to the lecture and answer questions 31-40.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 4. You will hear a lecture on urban planning. Look at questions 31 to 40.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Good morning everyone. Today we're going to discuss the concept of sustainable urban planning and how cities can be designed to be more environmentally friendly and livable. With more than half the world's population now living in urban areas, how we plan and manage cities has never been more important. Let me begin by defining what we mean by sustainable urban planning. Essentially, it's an approach that aims to create cities that meet the needs of current residents without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. This involves considering environmental, social, and economic factors. One key principle is mixed-use development. This means designing areas that combine residential, commercial, and recreational spaces, reducing the need for long commutes. Another important concept is green infrastructure - this includes parks, green roofs, and urban forests that help manage stormwater, reduce air pollution, and provide recreational space. Public transportation is also crucial. Well-designed public transport systems can significantly reduce car dependency and associated emissions. Many European cities like Copenhagen and Amsterdam are excellent examples of this, with extensive cycling networks and reliable public transport. Density is another consideration. Medium-density development is often more sustainable than both high-rise towers and low-density sprawl, as it supports public transport while maintaining human scale. Finally, community participation is vital. When local residents are involved in planning decisions, the results better reflect their needs and values. In our next lecture, we'll look at case studies of successful sustainable cities around the world.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of the lecture?
What percentage of world population lives in urban areas?
What does sustainable planning consider?
options: A) Environmental, social and economic factors

questions.questionText: What is mixed-use development?
options: B) Combining different functions in one area

questions.questionText: What is NOT mentioned as part of green infrastructure?
What can public transport reduce?
Which cities are given as good examples?
What type of density is often most sustainable?
Why is community participation important?
What will the next lecture cover?
options: B) Case studies of sustainable cities

passages.title: Passage 1: The History of Public Libraries
passages.content:
Public libraries have played a crucial role in education and community development for centuries. The concept of free access to books and knowledge dates back to ancient times, but modern public libraries as we know them began in the 19th century.

The first public library in the UK was established in Manchester in 1852, following the Public Libraries Act of 1850. This legislation allowed towns to raise taxes to support public libraries. Before this, libraries were mainly private institutions or belonged to universities and wealthy individuals.

In the United States, the first public library was founded in Boston in 1854. A significant figure in American library history was Andrew Carnegie, a wealthy industrialist who donated money to build over 2,500 libraries worldwide between 1883 and 1929.

The role of libraries has evolved significantly over time. Initially, they primarily served as repositories for books. Today, they function as community centers offering internet access, digital resources, educational programs, and meeting spaces. Many libraries now provide e-books, audiobooks, and online databases that users can access from home.

Despite the digital revolution, physical library visits remain popular. A recent survey showed that 50% of adults in the UK visited a library in the past year. Libraries continue to adapt to changing needs by offering services like computer classes, job search assistance, and children's reading programs.

Funding remains a challenge for many public libraries. They rely on a combination of government support, private donations, and fundraising events. Some libraries have introduced cafe areas or room rentals to generate additional income.

The future of public libraries looks promising as they continue to reinvent themselves. While the ways people access information have changed, the fundamental purpose of libraries - providing free access to knowledge and supporting learning - remains as important as ever.

questions.questionText: When was the first UK public library established?
What legislation enabled UK public libraries?
Where was the first US public library founded?
How many libraries did Andrew Carnegie help build?
What was the original main function of libraries?
What percentage of UK adults recently visited a library?
What modern services do libraries provide?
What challenge do libraries face?
How do some libraries generate extra income?
What remains unchanged about libraries?

passages.title: Passage 2: The Benefits of Bilingualism
passages.content:
Speaking more than one language offers numerous cognitive, social, and professional advantages. Research has shown that bilingual individuals often develop enhanced mental abilities compared to monolingual speakers.

One significant benefit is improved executive function. Bilingual people regularly practice switching between languages, which strengthens their ability to focus attention, ignore distractions, and switch between tasks. This mental flexibility can be advantageous in many aspects of life, from academic performance to problem-solving in the workplace.

Bilingualism also appears to delay the onset of age-related cognitive decline. Studies indicate that bilingual adults may develop dementia up to five years later than monolingual individuals. The constant mental exercise of managing two languages seems to build cognitive reserve that protects brain health in later life.

From a social perspective, bilingualism facilitates cross-cultural understanding and communication. People who speak multiple languages can connect with wider communities and access different cultural perspectives. This can lead to greater empathy and appreciation for diversity.

Professionally, bilingualism is increasingly valued in our globalized world. Many employers specifically seek candidates who can communicate with international clients or colleagues. Bilingual employees often have access to better job opportunities and may command higher salaries, particularly in fields like business, healthcare, and tourism.

Contrary to past beliefs, learning a second language does not confuse children or delay their development. In fact, research shows that bilingual children often develop stronger metalinguistic awareness - the ability to think about language as a system. This can actually support their literacy development in both languages.

While becoming fluent in a second language requires significant effort, the long-term benefits make it a worthwhile investment. Educational systems worldwide are increasingly recognizing these advantages and introducing second language instruction at earlier ages.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of the passage?
What mental ability is strengthened by bilingualism?
How much later might bilinguals develop dementia?
What social benefit is mentioned?
In which fields are bilingual employees particularly valued?
What was a past belief about bilingual children?
What do bilingual children develop strongly?
What is the author's view of learning a second language?
What are educational systems doing?
What builds cognitive reserve?

passages.title: Passage 3: Remote Work and Its Impact
passages.content:
The shift to remote work has been one of the most significant workplace transformations in recent history. Accelerated by the COVID-19 pandemic, remote work has become a permanent feature for many organizations worldwide.

Studies show that remote work can increase productivity for certain types of tasks. Employees often report fewer distractions and less time spent commuting, allowing them to focus more effectively on their work. However, this productivity boost depends on the nature of the work and the individual's home environment.

One major challenge of remote work is maintaining work-life balance. Without clear boundaries between professional and personal spaces, many remote workers struggle to 'switch off' at the end of the day. This can lead to longer working hours and increased stress levels.

Communication presents another significant challenge. While digital tools like video conferencing and messaging platforms help bridge the gap, they cannot fully replace face-to-face interaction. Many teams report difficulties with collaboration and innovation when working entirely remotely.

From an environmental perspective, remote work offers substantial benefits. Reduced commuting leads to lower carbon emissions and decreased traffic congestion. Companies can also reduce their office space requirements, leading to lower energy consumption and operational costs.

The economic impact of remote work is complex. While employees save money on commuting and work attire, they face increased household expenses for utilities and office equipment. Cities that rely on office workers may experience economic challenges as fewer people commute into business districts.

Looking forward, hybrid models that combine remote and office work appear to be the most popular solution. This approach offers flexibility while maintaining opportunities for in-person collaboration and social connection. Companies are redesigning office spaces to support this new way of working, with more meeting areas and fewer individual desks.

Successful remote work requires both organizations and employees to develop new skills and adapt to different ways of working. As technology continues to evolve, remote work will likely become even more integrated into our working lives.

questions.questionText: What accelerated the shift to remote work?
What productivity benefit is mentioned?
What is a major challenge of remote work?
What communication difficulty is mentioned?
What environmental benefit does remote work offer?
What household expenses increase with remote work?
What appears to be the most popular future solution?
How are companies redesigning offices?
What do both organizations and employees need to develop?
What is the author's view of remote work's future?
options: A) It will replace all office work
C) It will only continue in tech
D) It will become more integrated

tasks.title: Writing Task 1
tasks.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on this task. The chart below shows the percentage of households in a European country that owned different types of electronic devices between 2000 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.
tasks.description: You will be presented with a line graph showing household ownership of electronic devices over time.

sampleData.title: Household Ownership of Electronic Devices (2000-2020)
sampleData.description: Line graph showing changes in household ownership of four electronic devices over two decades

tasks.title: Writing Task 2
tasks.instructions: You should spend about 40 minutes on this task. Some people believe that governments should spend more money on public transportation systems rather than building new roads. Others think that improving road infrastructure is more important. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.
tasks.description: You will be presented with contrasting views on transportation funding and asked to discuss them.
keyPoints: Arguments for investing in public transportation

parts.title: Part 1: Introduction and Interview
parts.instructions: The examiner will ask you questions about yourself and familiar topics.
sampleQuestions: What do you like about your hometown?
What are you studying? / What work do you do?
How do you usually spend your weekends?
What kind of weather do you prefer?
Do you prefer hot or cold seasons? Why?

parts.title: Part 2: Individual Long Turn
parts.instructions: You will be given a topic and have one minute to prepare before speaking for 1-2 minutes.

taskCard.topic: Describe a skill you would like to learn.
points: Why you want to learn it
How it might be useful to you

parts.title: Part 3: Two-way Discussion
parts.instructions: The examiner will ask further questions connected to the topic in Part 2.
sampleQuestions: What skills are most useful for young people today?
How has technology changed the way people learn new skills?
Do you think practical skills or academic knowledge is more important?
Why do some people find it difficult to learn new skills?
How can schools better prepare students with practical skills?

metadata.author: IELTS Academic Testing System
metadata.notes: IELTS Academic Test for 24 November 2024 with realistic questions and topics. This test follows the actual IELTS format and includes common themes like accommodation, education, technology, and urban planning.
